The deadline for this problem was decades ago. Our government has always been reactive in addressing problems but proactive in stealing from our Treasury.

During the early 70’s, we have experienced the first global oil shock. Ever since , we have learned that OPEC will weaponize their oil products.

But our leaders have never learned to address the oil problem properly.

The oil deregulation program started on the wrong foot. The new entrants were never given the chance to have a head start to even the playing field. 

Even the weekly oil adjustment that was based on MOPS could not be scrutinized since it is confidential. Thus, there was no transparency.

If my memory serves me right, only Petron has a refinery while everyone imports their oil in finished products.

It is high time to review the oil deregulation law to address its flaws for the benefit of the future generation.

This is an important conversation. Professional accountability should never depend on public pressure alone.

Kung may malinaw na standards ang isang propesyon, dapat may malinaw ding paninindigan kapag nalalabag ang mga iyon. Respect and dignity—lalo na sa public discourse—should be the baseline, not the exception.

Many lawyers, including women in the profession, work every day to uphold those standards. Their work deserves an institution that speaks just as clearly when the moment calls for it.

Transparency and ethical leadership matter. Silence, especially in moments like this, inevitably raises hard questions.

#Accountability #RuleOfLaw #LegalEthics #RespectAndDignity #WomenInLaw #ProfessionalStandards #Philippines #PublicDiscourse

This is a really eye-opening breakdown. It’s surprising how much of our national security framework is still anchored in laws written generations ago.

Totoo—ibang mundo na ang espionage ngayon. Cyber operations, digital leaks, social engineering… hindi na ito katulad noong 1940s. Kung ganito na ka-advanced ang methods, kailangan talagang sumabay ang batas.

At the same time, stories like this also show how recruitment often targets financially vulnerable people. That doesn’t excuse the act, but it does show why stronger safeguards, better oversight, and clearer deterrence are important.

Hopefully lawmakers move faster on updating these laws. National security frameworks should evolve as fast as the threats they’re trying to address.

#NationalSecurity #EspionageLaw #PhilippineLaw #WestPhilippineSea #PublicPolicy #RuleOfLaw #Philippines #SecurityMatters

37.text:
With the political cauldron exploding above boiling point, fake news now abounds. More than during the covid crisis, the law against disinformation and false news needs its ax to fall on those purveyors pretending to inform but actually misleading the general public.

https://www.facebook.com/share/p/q5BvegCfjJecLsRn/

What's authorities doing now? The homeland's national security is in clear and present danger with destabilizing and serious threats to our country. Too much trolls, many posing as vloggers

What's our intelligence and national security agencies doing? It's not a matter of budget, but of deployment and efficiency, hopefully not corruption or skills.

Paid hacks and trolls are abusing our democratic space, they should be exposed and prosecuted.

"To report fake news and other cybercrimes, contact the PNP Anti-Cybercrime Group at (+63) 998 598 8116 or through their official Facebook account at @anticybercrimegroup"

"Citizens caught peddling fake news could be charged for violating Republic Act 10175 (Anti-Cybercrime Law), or under Section 6 (f) of Republic Act 11469 (Bayanihan to Heal As One Act), whichever is applicable."

https://www.covid19.gov.ph/information/dismiss-disinformation

Good analysis. The damage is real.

But maybe the core problem isn’t just enforcement — it’s design.

We’re using laws meant for individual misconduct, like the Cybercrime Prevention Act of 2012 and the Safe Spaces Act, to fight coordinated influence networks operating at scale.

That’s like regulating a factory by arresting one worker at a time.

If the harm is systemic, the law has to evolve intentionally — not stretch five different statutes to cover something they were never built to handle.

Otherwise, we stay reactive while the machinery stays intact.

Kung hindi tarantado gobyerno natin sa mga IT personnels nila na halos sukuan na ang public service dito, edi matino government websites natin at napreprevent ang ganitong mga modus ng troll farm, literal na nagtransition din mga sindikato maliban sa online lending into a DDS disinformation peddlers sa comments as side gigs din kundi sila mismo yung "vloggers".

Yes the Russian army who have been committing war crimes/also believers in disinformation opns/messages.

I think the most effective solution is to have a strict law that heavilly penalize big social platforms from spreading malicious and fake information. This will force these platforms to fact check their contents.

39.text:
I said before that third degree would be the best compromise.
Am I wrong or naive?
Thank you, Sir and to your follower. Ang ganda at ang linaw ng pagkalatag. Sana patuloy po tayong magmamatyag at magingay sa mga mahahalagang issues.
Seems to me that SB1901 is like "consuelo de Bobo".
Our Lord Jesus Christ, dismantle the political dynasties in our country and lead us towards Your righteous governance.
Meron kaya online petition para dito?

Mahirap talaga maging mambabatas kapag alam mong majority ay against sa gagawin mo. The only thing I like about this is somehow may precedent na. Then, we can go forward. And hoping na mabilis lang ang amendment to the preferred version natin.
This is not an incremental solution, it's a Showbiz solution.
Sen. Hontiveros set a stronger bill aside to sponsor Sen. Robin Padilla's version. 😕 Is that what twelve senators compromised on?

Good post Morning Coffee Thoughts 2.0 and explains the differences. SB 1548 is considered a danger to the special interests clans/cliques/hugpongs just like the Rome Statute for the PH being a member is a danger to autocratic governance liking said clans/cliques/hugpongs.

The "Kita tayo sa Hong Kong joyrider"/oath breaker and his clan would not agree or support Sen Risa's SB 1548, that is why his son passed also an "anti dynasty daw" bill also.

This government has spent years ignoring the Constitution, and now they want us to celebrate this current version at face value? It's a classic bait-and-switch. By limiting the ban to the 2nd degree and allowing 'jurisdiction jumping', they aren't ending dynasties - they're just providing a manual on how to keep in power. It's a law designed to look like reform while still protecting the 'fat dynasties' that already run the country. We shouldn't be gullible enough to call this progress when it's actually just a survival strategy for the political elites.

Don't get me wrong, I agree that we need a necessary reform. But tbh, with the political dynasty cemented themselves in the legislative, any bill that targeted their political interest, is considered a threat, and they see it into the SB 1548.

Even Senator Hontiveros sees the optics and the grim reality that she realizes that her own bill, wouldn't work on a establish political culture so she go on the moderate path, show a bill carrying such reform but not too powerful to agitate political families in the house and the senate.

She will treat the SB 1548 as back-up so when the bicameral arrives she can suggest some of its clause to add in the unified version.
At the end of the day, all of them are still protecting their own interests. Thank you for all involved in shedding light to this matter.
